coredata.dc:title: Construction and photocatalysis of carbon quantum dots/layered mesoporous titanium dioxide (CQDs/LM-TiO2) composites
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Li-Yuan
Han, Yan-Lin
Yang, Jin-Ju
Deng, Sheng-Lian
Wang, Bi-Yu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid as a carbon source. Layered mesoporous titanium dioxide (LM-TiO2) was synthesized by the inorganic precipitation-peptization method using titanium sulfate as the titanium source, ammonia as the precipitant and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) as the template. Subsequently, CQDs and LM-TiO2 were compounded to obtain carbon quantum dots/layered mesoporous titanium dioxide composites (CQDs/LM-TiO2). The photocatalytic performance of the samples was studied by using methyl orange as the target organic pollutant. The results indicated that the addition of CTAB led to a layered mesoporous structure, reduced crystallinity and inhibited the crystal growth of TiO2. Carbonaceous groups were detected on the surface of LM-TiO2, thus confirming the formation of CQDs/LM-TiO2 composites. The light absorption intensity of LM-TiO2 was greatly enhanced in the ultraviolet range compared with simple TiO2 and that of CQDs/LM-TiO2 was improved in the visible light region. The threshold wavelength of CQDs/LM-TiO2 also shows a certain redshift compared with that of LM-TiO2. The degradation rate of methyl orange by 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 was 91.04% after irradiation for 24 min, which was significantly higher than that of simple TiO2 and LM-TiO2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanium dioxide
Carbon quantum dots
Inorganic precipitation-peptization method
Surfactant
Photocatalysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Youth Project of Neijiang Normal University (2020QN16) and the Undergraduate Innovative Project of Neijiang Normal University (X2020023).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of different TiO2 samples. (a) TiO2, (b) LM-TiO2, (c) CQDs/LM-TiO2.
XRD patterns of different proportions of CQDs/LM-TiO2. (a) TiO2, (b) 0 wt%, (c) 0.1 wt% (d) 0.2 wt% and (e) 0.3 wt%
Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of simple TiO2, LM-TiO2 and CQDs/LM-TiO2 at various compositions. (a) Simple TiO2, (b) 0 wt%, (c) 0.1 wt%, (d) 0.2 wt% and (e) 0.3 wt%
Infrared spectra of TiO2, LM-TiO2 and CQDs/LM-TiO2
Adsorption isotherms and pore size distributions of different samples. (a) Simple TiO2, (b) LM-TiO2 and (c) 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2
XPS spectra of different LM-TiO2 and 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2. (a) Survey, (b) Ti 2p peaks, (c) O 1s and O 2s peaks, (d) C 1 s peaks, (e) 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 C 1s peaks fitting curves
Degradation rate of methyl orange by TiO2 and CQDs/LM-TiO2 at different CQDs/LM-TiO2 weight ratios. (a) Blank group, (b) TiO2, (c) 0 wt%, (d) 0.1 wt%, (e) 0.2 wt% and (f) 0.3 wt%.
Schematic diagram of the preparation of CQDs/LM-TiO2 and mechanistic diagram of photocatalysis on the layered mesoporous structure and CQDs-modified TiO2
. Grain sizes of TiO2, LM-TiO2 and CQDs/LM-TiO2.
. The pore structure data of simple TiO2, LM-TiO2 and 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2.
. Adsorption of methyl orange by TiO2 and CQDs/LM-TiO2.

head.ce:title: Construction and photocatalysis of carbon quantum dots/layered mesoporous titanium dioxide (CQDs/LM-TiO2) composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Neijiang Normal University, Neijiang 641112, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Neijiang Normal University, Neijiang 641112, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Office of Financial Affairs, Neijiang Normal University, Neijiang 641112, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Office of Financial Affairs, Neijiang Normal University, Neijiang 641112, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid as a carbon source. Layered mesoporous titanium dioxide (LM-TiO2) was synthesized by the inorganic precipitation-peptization method using titanium sulfate as the titanium source, ammonia as the precipitant and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) as the template. Subsequently, CQDs and LM-TiO2 were compounded to obtain carbon quantum dots/layered mesoporous titanium dioxide composites (CQDs/LM-TiO2). The photocatalytic performance of the samples was studied by using methyl orange as the target organic pollutant. The results indicated that the addition of CTAB led to a layered mesoporous structure, reduced crystallinity and inhibited the crystal growth of TiO2. Carbonaceous groups were detected on the surface of LM-TiO2, thus confirming the formation of CQDs/LM-TiO2 composites. The light absorption intensity of LM-TiO2 was greatly enhanced in the ultraviolet range compared with simple TiO2 and that of CQDs/LM-TiO2 was improved in the visible light region. The threshold wavelength of CQDs/LM-TiO2 also shows a certain redshift compared with that of LM-TiO2. The degradation rate of methyl orange by 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 was 91.04% after irradiation for 24 min, which was significantly higher than that of simple TiO2 and LM-TiO2.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of society, water pollution is becoming increasingly serious. To enable green sustainable development, water pollution prevention and control has become a topic of interest in green chemistry research in recent years. There are many complex, toxic and persistent organic pollutants in modern industrial wastewater. The technologies of treating organic pollutants in wastewater mainly include electrochemical catalytic technology [1], ultrasonic degradation [2] and photocatalytic technology [3–5]. However, due to the technical limitations and high energy consumption, they are limited in practical application. Among them, photocatalytic technology has promise, being a way to degrade pollutants into H2O, CO2 and other inorganic substances via photocatalytic reactions, which have low energy consumption and no harmful waste byproducts. Semiconductors as photocatalysts have become a research focus in recent years because of their excellent photocatalytic performance. Semiconductor catalysts include TiO2, ZnO, ZnS, CdS, among others [6–11]. TiO2 has attracted the attention of researchers due to its low cost, nontoxicity, high stability and photocatalytic activity. TiO2 is widely used in water pollution treatment, photoelectric conversion, energy storage and photocatalytic hydrogen production [12–16].

The band gap of TiO2 is 3.2 eV, which is equivalent to light with a wavelength of 387 nm; only at shorter wavelengths can the electrons on the dielectric band be excited to transition to the conduction band, forming highly active electrons and holes. The holes can capture the electrons of OH– or H2O on the material surface and generate
OH, which is a strong oxidant. The electrons can adsorb O2 on the material surface to form the active superoxide radical
O2 – and then produce the free radicals
HO2 and
OH. The produced
OH can then degrade organic or inorganic pollutants into inorganic small molecules, CO2 and H2O. However, the utilization of sunlight by TiO2 is only 3–5%, and the photogenerated electrons and holes easily recombine, which reduces its photocatalytic activity and limits its application. To improve the utilization rate of sunlight, TiO2 is often modified by doping metal and nonmetallic ions, creating a special morphology, adding semiconductor compounds, sensitizing quantum dot, depositing noble metals and other methodsetc [17–22].

Quantum dot sensitization can improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2. However, most quantum dots are toxic heavy metal ions that are harmful to the environment. In recent years, a new kind of carbon nanomaterial quantum dot (CQDs) with fluorescence properties has been found. The diameter of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is less than 10 nm and is composed of quasidiscrete spherical carbon nanoparticles. CQDs have the advantages of wide sources, low cost, stable physical and chemical properties, nontoxicity and easy functionalized modification. The photocatalytic performance of TiO2 can be improved after forming a compound with carbon quantum dots due to the following effects: (1). The near-infrared properties of CQDs broaden the visible light absorption range of TiO2 [23]; (2). CQDs’ photoluminescence conversion characteristics make TiO2 produce more photogenerated electrons and holes [24]; (3). CQDs have a strong electron transfer property, which reduces the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes and generates more active sites [25,26]. Due to these characteristics of carbon quantum dots, the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 can be effectively improved via CQDs sensitization. Therefore, CQDs have broad prospects in the field of photocatalysis.

At present, CQDs have been widely studied in the field of photocatalysis. Gholinejad et al. [27] synthesized magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles with carboxyl and hydroxyl groups and used them for the reduction of Pd2+ and the formation of Pd nanoparticles. Cheng et al. [28] prepared graphene film-modified TiO2 nanotube array photoelectrodes by the anodic oxidation method and found that CQDs increased the absorption ability of TiO2 nanotubes to visible light and thus improved photocatalytic activity. Song et al. [29] prepared N-doped CQDs and TiO2 microspheres by a one-step synthesis method. Ti-O-C bonds appeared in the characterization analysis, which confirmed the connection between carbon dots and titanium dioxide. The materials showed a high activity in photocatalytic reactions, illustrating that the synergistic effect of quantum dots and heterophases enhanced the photocatalytic performance. Tang et al. [30] prepared CQDs-TiO2 composites with titanyl sulfate as the titanium source and graphite rods electrolyzed under a hydrothermal reaction. CQDs can be successfully modified on anatase and brookite titanium dioxide. As an electronic storage medium, CQDs can improve charge separation, inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs and increase hydrogen production.

Hu et al. [31] synthesized CQD/BiOCl/BiOBr composite nanosheet photocatalysts through an in situ ionic liquid induction strategy and confirmed that the introduction of CQDs can simultaneously improve visible light absorption and separation efficiency of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. Electron spin resonance and radical quenching experiments showed that holes and superoxide radicals are the main factors involved in the degradation of pollutants in the photocatalytic reaction. The excellent physical and chemical properties of CQDs provide an effective way to improve the photocatalytic performance of semiconductors.

To the best of knowledge, the preparation methods of titanium dioxide mainly include hydrothermal method, inorganic precipitation-peptization method, solvothermal technology, sol-gel process and others. As reported, an effective way to improve photocatalytic performance is controlling the morphology of titanium dioxide. Most researchers mainly use hydrothermal methods to change the morphology of TiO2, such as spherical and fibrous shapes. Compared with the traditional method, the inorganic precipitation-peptization method has the advantages of a simple process, low energy consumption and easy industrial production. CTAB can construct a special morphology and structure of TiO2 in the preparation process, which can effectively improve the performance of photocatalysts. The hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs with citric acid as the raw material is a common technology that has been used in various fields. However, its development and application in the field of photocatalysis are far from sufficient. We found that the combination of CQDs and LM-TiO2 can significantly improve the photocatalytic activity of LM-TiO2. Inourpreviousstudy, several other carbon sources were also used to prepare CQDs, however, the effect was not as good as when using citric acid. To the best of our knowledge, the structure and photocatalytic properties of carbon quantum dot/layered mesoporous titanium dioxide (CQDs/LM-TiO2) composites are rare.

In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. Layered mesoporous (LM-TiO2) was synthesized by the inorganic precipitation-peptization method with CTAB as the morphology control agent, followed by compounding with CQDs to obtain CQDs/LM-TiO2. Methyl orange was used to simulate organic pollutants in wastewater, and the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/LM-TiO2 was studied. The CQDs/LM-TiO2 composites can be used in the degradation of organic pollutants in sewage, have the advantages of low cost, nontoxicity, good stability and photocatalytic performance and can greatly improve the utilization rate of light. CQDs/LM-TiO2 composites have good development prospects in sewage treatment.

According to the improved method in reference [32], citric acid (3.0 g) was dissolved by 45 mL of deionized water in a 100 mL beaker and the solution was transferred to a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor, followed by placing in a drying oven at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the CQDs solution was cooled to room temperature to obtain a light yellow, transparent solution. Then, centrifugation was performed at a speed of 4000 r·min−1 for 10 min to remove the unreacted large particles, and dialysis was carried out in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 3500 Da for 24 h. After dialysis, the carbon quantum dot (CQDs) solution was obtained.

Ti(SO4)2 (4.8 g) was added to a beaker and then dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water by continuous stirring for 15 min, followed by slow addition of ammonia buffer (about one drop every 3 s) to adjust the pH to 8–9 until obtaining a white precipitate, followed by aging for 1 h. Subsequently, the precipitate was centrifugally washed with deionized water 4–5 times to remove impurity ions. After that, the white sediment was transferred to the beaker, 100 mL of deionized water was added with stirring for 15 min to mix evenly, and then 0.09 g of CTAB was added, followed by stirring for 20 min. Finally, 8.4 mL of 30% H2O2 was slowly dropped into the solution (about one dropper every 10 s). After that, a transparent yellow solution was obtained by agitation at room temperature for 1 h.

The prepared colloids were aged for 12 h and dried in an oven at 80 °C for 10 h. Then, the samples were ground into powders and placed in a crucible. Finally, the samples were calcined at 550 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace with a heating rate of 2 °C/min and then cooled in the furnace. Simple TiO2 powders were obtained without adding CTAB.

Then, 0.5 g TiO2 powder was placed into a beaker, and one of either 0.1 wt%, 0.2 wt% or 0.3 wt% CQDs solutions (with a mass ratio of citric acid to TiO2) were added. Then, 20 mL of deionized water was added with ultrasonication for 30 min and magnetic stirring for 3 h to mix evenly. Subsequently, the solution was dried in an oven at 80 °C for 10 h, centrifugally washed with deionized water 4 times and dried again to obtain CQDs/LM-TiO2.
LM-TiO2 and CQDs/LM-TiO2 can be prepared by the methods mentioned above, and the yields of LM-TiO2 and CQDs/LM-TiO2 are calculated to be 80.7% and 72.6% respectively.

Methyl orange solution (30 mL, 20 mg/L) was put into a quartz tube, and 0.02 g of photocatalyst was added. Then, the quartz tube was placed in a photochemical reactor. The mixture was first kept in the dark for 30 min and then centrifuged for 10 min. The absorbance was then measured and designated as the initial absorbance (A0). After that, the photochemical reactor was turned on, and the power of the metal halide lamp was adjusted (wavelength range: 280–780 nm) to 300 W to enable photocatalytic degradation. The absorbance (At) was measured at regular intervals, and the degradation rate (X) was calculated by formula (1) below. The photocatalytic performance of the sample was reflected by the degradation rate. The methyl orange solution was used to simulate organic substances in sewage, and the photocatalytic performance of the sample was evaluated using a photochemical reactor with an eight-position magnetic stirring reactor (BL-GHX-V, Shanghai Bilon Instrument Co., Ltd., China). In the photochemical reactor, the metal halide lamp was placed in a quartz cold well in the middle, and the power of the lamp was adjusted according to need. The absorbance of methyl orange solution was determined by an ultraviolet–visible Shanghai Xinmao Instrument Co., Ltd., China).
(1)
X =
A 0
-
A t
/
A 0
× 100 %

The surface morphology of the samples was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, VEGA3, TESCAN, Czech Republic). The crystal phase composition of the samples was investigated with X-ray diffraction (DX-2700, Dandong Haoyuan Instrument Co., Ltd., China) using Cu Kα radiation at a scanning rate of 0.05° s−1 and a working voltage and current of 40 kV/30 mA. The ultraviolet absorption bands of the samples were analyzed by an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV–Vis-Abs, UV2700, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). The functional groups of the samples were revealed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, WQF-510A, Beijing Beifen-Ruili Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., China). The pore size and specific surface area were measured on a surface area analyzer (Autosorb iQ2, Quantachrome, USA) using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The elemental composition and valence state of the sample were characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250 Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

Fig. 1 shows SEM images of TiO2, LM-TiO2 and CQDs/LM-TiO2. From Fig. 1, the simple TiO2 prepared by the precipitation-peptization method is a particle with a smooth surface, similar particle sizes and irregular blocky-shaped. The particle size of the sample modified by CTAB, namely, LM-TiO2, is similar to that of pure TiO2, has a special layered structure, and the surface of the sample is rougher accompanied by the uniform distribution of particles, which increases the specific surface area of the sample. Because the addition of CTAB inhibits the activation effect of the surface sol of titanium dioxide, forming a stable micelle, TiO2 forms a layered structure with hydrophilic groups at the top and hydrophobic groups at the bottom under sol conditions. After drying and calcination, layered titanium dioxide with a rough surface is obtained due to the decomposition of CTAB.

The morphology of CQDs/LM-TiO2 does not change significantly compared to LM-TiO2, but the lamellar morphology is reduced. In the composite process, the lamellar morphology of LM-TiO2 is partially destroyed by dissolution, magnetic stirring, centrifugation and drying, and more lamellar particles are produced. The specific surface area of CQDs/LM-TiO2 is increased, which could provide more active sites for photocatalytic reactions. The diameter of the CQDs is approximately several nanometers, which has no obvious effect on the morphology of LM-TiO2.

Fig. 2 shows the X-ray diffraction patterns of different CQDs/LM-TiO2 samples calcined at 550 °C. According to the standard cards (ICDD PDF#21-1272, ICDD PDF#21-1276), the diffraction peaks of anatase titanium dioxide (101), (004), (200), (105), (211) and (204) are at 2θ = 25.3°, 37.8°, 48.1°, 53.8°, 55.2° and 62.8° respectively. From the curves in Fig. 2, only the diffraction peaks of anatase titanium dioxide appear in the samples, and no peaks of the rutile phase are detected, which indicates that only anatase titanium dioxide is obtained when calcined at this temperature. There is no diffraction peak related to CTAB in the curve a, b, c and d. The reason is that the melting point of CTAB is approximately 237–250 °C, so CTAB decomposes when calcined at 550 °C. From curve a and b, the addition of CTAB weakens the intensity and broadens the width of the diffraction peaks, which shows that CTAB can reduce the crystallinity and inhibit the crystal growth of TiO2. Because CTAB forms micelles which hinder the migration of TiO2 grain boundary and inhibit the grain growth. Additionally, no diffraction peak of CQDs is found in curve c, d, and e. In addition, the diffraction peaks of LM-TiO2 are slightly enhanced with increasing proportion of CQDs, while the influence on the diffraction peak width is weak. This indicates that most CQDs cover the surface of LM-TiO2 uniformly and not enter the lattice of LM-TiO2, and thus have little effect on the crystallization degree. The grain size of the sample can be calculated according to the Scherrer formula (2) and the characteristic diffraction peak, as shown in Table 1 . CTAB reduces the grain size of TiO2 and inhibits the growth of TiO2 grains. However, forming a composite of CQDs and TiO2 changes little of the crystal size of LM-TiO2 because CQDs do not participate in the nucleation and growth of TiO2 particles.
(2)
D = K λ /
β cos θ
nm

Fig. 3 shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs/LM-TiO2 with various ratios at 550 °C. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs/LM-TiO2 marked with a, b, c, d and e in Fig. 3 correspond to simple TiO2, 0%, 0.1 wt%, 0.2 wt% and 0.3 wt%, respectively. From Fig. 3a and b, compared with TiO2, the absorption intensity of LM-TiO2 in the ultraviolet region is greatly enhanced; however, the absorption intensity shows some reduction in the visible region, which is due to the layered mesoporous structure of LM-TiO2. With this structure, the large specific surface area can provide more reactive sites, which can promote the absorption of light. However, the band gap energy difference between LM-TiO2 and TiO2 is quite small, which is the main factor for the low absorption intensity in the visible region. Compared with LM-TiO2, the absorption intensity of CQDs/LM-TiO2 in the ultraviolet region decreased because the LUMO energy level of CQDs is higher than that of LM-TiO2, and some photogenerated electrons of CQDs are transferred to the conduction band of LM-TiO2 that is at a lower energy level, which suppresses the light absorption intensity of CQDs [33,34].

In addition, from Fig. 3c, d and e, the absorption intensity first increases and then decreases with increasing CQDs content. In the visible region, the absorption intensity of CQDs/LM-TiO2 is higher than that of LM-TiO2. However, with the increase in the amount of CQDs, the absorption in the visible light region does not obviously change. When the excess CQDs are distributed on the surface of LM-TiO2, the carbon quantum dots will aggregate, occupy a large number of photocatalytic active sites on the surface, and promote the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, which results in reduced visible light absorption. However, the combination of an appropriate amount of carbon quantum dots can effectively promote the redshift of the absorption wavelength due to its photoluminescence performance and the synergistic effect between CQDs and titanium dioxide. Qu et al. [35] prepared graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and GQDs/TiO2 nanotube composites by a simple hydrothermal method, and the composites showed photoluminescence characteristics of up and downconversion. The synergistic effect of the upconversion performance of GQDs enhances visible light absorption and effectively inhibits the recombination of electrons and holes, which also confirms the analysis mentioned above. The threshold wavelengths (λg) of the absorption spectra of simple TiO2, LM-TiO2 and 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 particles are 407.11 nm, 407.59 nm, and 415.18 nm, respectively, which is due to the near-infrared absorption characteristics of CQDs.
(3)
E g
= 1240 /
λ g
eV

After modification using CQDs, the absorption band edge of the samples shows a redshift and broadening of the light absorption range, thus indicating improved photocatalytic performance. The band gap energy of different samples can be calculated by using formula (3). The results show that the band gap energies of simple TiO2, LM-TiO2 and 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 are 3.05 eV, 3.04 eV, and 2.99 eV, respectively. The band gap energy of anatase TiO2 is approximately 3.2 eV, which indicates that the band gap of TiO2 prepared by the inorganic precipitation-peptization method can be effectively reduced, and that the introduction of carbon quantum dots also reduces the band gap of LM-TiO2. This is because the carbon hybrid TiO2 disorders the intermediate band structure and produces impurity energy levels, which narrow the band gap of titanium dioxide and cause a redshift of the absorption band edge, thus enhancing visible light absorption by TiO2 [36,37].

Fig. 4 shows the FTIR spectra of TiO2, LM-TiO2 and CQDs/LM-TiO2 samples. In the FTIR spectra, the peak at 3600–3300 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of –OH on the TiO2 surface, the peak at 1600 cm−1 is caused by the bending vibration of water molecules, and the peak at 465 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the Ti-O-Ti bond. For LM-TiO2, the stretching vibration peak of the Csp3-H bond (–CH3, –CH2-) appears at 2840–2910 cm−1, and the stretching vibration peak of the C-N bond appears at 2350 cm−1, which may be attributed to the residual material after calcination of CTAB at high temperature. In addition, the stretching vibration peak intensity of the Ti-O-Ti bond at 465 cm−1 is higher on LM-TiO2 than on TiO2, which may be related to the increased distance between atoms and the increased dipole moment affected by CTAB.

In the FTIR spectra of CQDs/LM-TiO2, the symmetric stretching vibration peak of the C=C bond at 1400 cm−1 and the vibration absorption band of C=O at 1633 cm−1, which both belong to the stretching vibration peaks of carbon quantum dots [38] are detected. This indicates that CQDs were successfully compounded on LM-TiO2. The absorption intensities of the peaks at 3600–3300 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 are higher than those of TiO2 and LM-TiO2 because CQDs can promote the formation of photogenerated electrons and holes, enhance the charge diversion rate, transfer electrons to the interface of TiO2 and CQDs, and leave holes inside titanium dioxide. In other words, CQDs help charge separation and promote the production of
OH on the sample surface, which is conducive to improving the photocatalytic efficiency. In summary, FTIR analysis indicates that carbonaceous groups were introduced on the surface of LM-TiO2, which confirmed the formation of the CQDs/LM-TiO2 composite.

Fig. 5 presents N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distributions of TiO2, LM-TiO2 and 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2. Fig. 5 shows that the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm curves of TiO2 and LM-TiO2 samples form hysteresis loops at a relative pressure of P/P0 = 0.2 and show the characteristic type IV H2 adsorption–desorption, which reflects that the pore size distribution is relatively uniform and that the pore structure of the samples is complex. There may be typical “ink bottle” holes, tubular pores with uneven pore diameter distribution and interstitial pores with dense spherical particles. The N2 adsorption desorption isotherm of 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 also belongs to type IV H2, but there is no closed hysteresis loop. The phenomenon in Fig. 5c may be due to the insufficient degassing of CQDs on the surface of LM-TiO2, which makes the adsorption curve move down and the adsorption capacity decrease.

From Fig. 5a, it is confirmed the pore size distribution of TiO2 is uniform with a wide and high peak. The pore sizes are mainly 30–87 nm, and the pores are mainly 56 nm macropores and a small amount of mesopores. From Fig. 5b, the pore size distribution of LM-TiO2 is in the range of 3–10 nm. Uniform mesopores are formed, and the number of mesopores is increased compared to TiO2, which indicates that the incorporation of CTAB can effectively promote the formation of mesopores and inhibit the generation of macropores. In addition, the most pore size of TiO2 is 56 nm, and that of LM-TiO2 is 6 nm. Wang et al. [39] confirmed this observation by hydrothermal synthesis of mesoporous hydroxyapatite. This behavior is because CTAB, as a cationic surfactant, is composed of a positively charged hydrophilic head and hydrophobic tail with weak affinity to water molecules. CTAB has the properties of wetting, solubilization, emulsification and interfacial hydrolysis, and the critical micelle size of the surfactant is very small, ranging from 0.001 to 0.002 mol/dm3. When the critical micelle concentration is reached, spherical, claviform and layered micelles are formed. In addition, the wetting action of the surfactant can effectively promote the formation of special lamellar TiO2 and produce a large number of mesopores.

The pore size distribution of 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 in Fig. 5c shows that the pore sizes of the composite are 25–80 nm, and the majority aperture is approximately 49 nm. However, the pore sizes increase and the number of pores decreases as compared with LM-TiO2. This phenomenon is because the sizes of the CQDs are less than 10 nm, and a large number of CQDs accumulate or enter the mesopores, blocking part of the mesoporous structure. This also confirmed the conclusion that some CQDs were attached to the surface of LM-TiO2 in the XRD analysis and thus affected the photocatalytic performance of the catalyst.

As shown in Table 2 , many mesoporous structures are formed in LM-TiO2, which increases the specific surface area. Relatively speaking, the specific surface area of LM-TiO2 is greatly increased due to the composite of CQDs. First, the sizes of the CQDs are less than 10 nm, which can enter the mesopores of LM-TiO2, increasing the pore volume and pore diameter of 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2. In addition, CQDs have a unique nanospherical structure with a large specific surface area. Also, the introduction of carbon leads to the formation of porous structures [40]. These factors all significantly increase the specific surface area of CQDs/LM-TiO2.

The surface elemental composition and valence state of the CQDs/LM-TiO2 composites were analyzed by XPS characterization, as shown in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6a, b, c, and d show the full XPS spectra of the samples, the spectra of Ti, O and C, respectively, and Fig. 6e is the curve fits of C 1s peaks in 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2. According to Fig. 6a, LM-TiO2 and 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 are mainly composed of Ti, O and C. C in the former sample is caused by the carbon pollutant introduced in the photocatalysis test, while in the latter specimen, part of C is the carbon pollutant and the rest is the carbon intrinsic to CQDs, and the C spectrum is corrected.

From the XPS spectra of Ti in Fig. 6b, the binding energy of the spin orbital of Ti 2p3/2 in LM-TiO2 is 458.77 eV, which corresponds to the +3 oxidation state, and the binding energy of Ti 2p1/2 is 464.54 eV, which belongs to the +4 oxidation state, indicating that Ti in the sample mainly exists as Ti4+ [41,42]. The binding energy of Ti in LM-TiO2 is greater than that in 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2. Compared with LM-TiO2, the binding energies of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 in 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 decreased by 0.13 eV and 0.12 eV, respectively, illustrating that CQDs changed the chemical bond environment around Ti. This observation can be attributed to two causes. On the one hand, the introduction of C makes part of the Ti4+ reduce to Ti3+; on the other hand, C atoms enter the lattice of titanium dioxide to form Ti-C bonds, which increases the electron density around Ti atoms and reduces the binding energy.

Fig. 6c shows the XPS spectra of O. The binding energy of the O 1s orbital in 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 is reduced by 0.18 eV compared with that of LM-TiO2. In 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2, C atoms enter the lattice of titanium dioxide to form O-Ti-C bonds. In addition, the peak at 531.9 eV corresponds to
OH or C-O species, which are due to the replacement of Ti atoms in the lattice by C atoms to form Ti-O-C bonds, increasing the electron density around the O atom and reducing the binding energy of O.

In Fig. 6d, the peak positions of C 1s in LM-TiO2 were 284.83 eV and 288.65 eV, while those in 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 were 284.76 eV and 288.74 eV, respectively, a decrease of 0.07 eV and 0.09 eV, respectively. This may be due to the influence of CQDs on the peak of C 1s. The peak intensity is stronger at 288.74 eV because of the rich C=O functional groups in CQDs, which further indicates the presence of CQDs in the sample.

Fig. 6e shows the fitting curves of the C spectrum in 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2. The peaks at 284.82, 286.14 and 288.56 eV correspond to C-C, C-O, and C=O and COO– species, respectively [43], which is consistent with the FTIR analysis. In addition, faint peaks at 282.15 eV and 280.86 eV are detected due to the O-Ti-C bond, which indicates that a small amount of C replaces oxygen or oxygen vacancies in the TiO2 lattice [44]. The introduction of the impurity energy level in the TiO2 band gap causes a redshift of the light absorption band edge, while the substitution of Ti by C does not affect the visible light absorption [45]. However, most CQDs are mainly distributed on the surface of TiO2 in the form of surface deposits or covering, which further confirms the XRD analysis.

The XPS quantitative analysis shows that the elemental composition of LM-TiO2 is Ti, O and C, and the content of each element is 25.16 at.%, 53.10 at.%, and 21.74 at.%, respectively. The elemental composition of 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 is Ti, O and C, and the content of each element is 26.36 at.%, 54.62 at.%, and 19.02 at.%, respectively. The O/Ti ratio of LM-TiO2 is 2.11 and that of the 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 sample is 2.07, both of which are larger than the stoichiometric ratio of simple TiO2 of 2:1, indicating that the addition of CTAB causes an increase in the O/Ti ratio. On the one hand, the layered mesoporous structure can enhance the ability of light scattering and trapping, generate more photogenerated electrons and holes with a high activity, and produce more ·OH with strong oxidation potential. On the other hand, a larger specific surface area can increase the adsorption of water and hydroxyl groups on the sample surface, which results in abundant OH on the sample surface and the increased ratio of O/Ti in the sample, as proven by FTIR analysis[46]. The O/Ti ratio of 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 decreased compared with that of LM-TiO2 because the coverage of CQDs on the surface of LM-TiO2 reduced the amount of
OH exposed on the surface [47,48].

In the photocatalytic performance evaluation, samples were treated in the dark for 30 min to obtain the adsorption data shown in Table 3 . According to the data in Table 3, after dark treatment, the absorbance of the solution has almost no change, indicating that the TiO2 photocatalyst has no obvious adsorption of methyl orange. After photochemical reaction, the decolorization rate of the sample was calculated by formula (1), and the results are shown in Fig. 7 . From Fig. 7, for CQDs/LM-TiO2 with different weight ratios, methyl orange has different degradation rates under the same illumination conditions for 24 min as follows: 0.2 wt% > 0.3 wt% > 0 wt% > 0.1 wt% > TiO2 > blank. Among them, the degradation effect of 0 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 is better than that of simple TiO2, which can be attributed to the unique layered mesoporous structure, which increases the specific surface area of the sample, improves the photocatalytic activity of semiconductor titanium dioxide, and enhances the degradation rate of methyl orange. The decolorization rate of methyl orange was highest in the case of 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2, reaching 91.04%, while the degradation rates of methyl orange by 0.3 wt% and 0.1 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 were only 85.32% and 44.04%, respectively. The results show that the photocatalytic performance of LM-TiO2 can be promoted by adding a certain amount of CQDs; however, excessive CQDs can accelerate the recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes on the catalyst surface, thus inhibiting the photocatalytic activity.

In our previous study [49], commercial P25 was used to degrade methyl orange under the same conditions, and the result indicated that the degradation rates of methyl orange by P25 was 93.26% after illumination for 24 min under the same conditions. The photocatalytic activities of P25 and 0.2 wt%CQDs/LM-TiO2 were well-matched. On the one hand, the particle size of P25 was significantly smaller than that of 0.2 wt%CQDs/LM-TiO2. The average particle size of P25 was 25 nm, while that of 0.2 wt%CQDs/LM-TiO2 was 2–5 μm. As known, the smaller the particle size, the larger the specific surface area, and the larger the contact area with methyl orange solution, which resulted in the better photocatalytic activity. On the other hand, P25 is composed by a mixed phases of anatase and rutile (with a mass ratio of 88.6% and 11.4%, respectively) [49]. Miyagi et al. [50] believed that the existence of moderate rutile phase in anatase phase can effectively promote the transfer of electrons and holes, and the separation and migration on the surface of titanium dioxide, which is conducive to the formation of ·OH, thus improving the photocatalytic performance. In this work, the particle size of 0.2 wt%CQDs/LM-TiO2 is significantly larger than that of P25 and is composed by single anatase phase TiO2. However, the photocatalytic activity of 0.2 wt%CQDs/LM-TiO2 is similar to that of P25, which indicates that the efficiency of nano-sized mixed crystals TiO2 (P25) can be achieved by a simple and low-cost preparation method in this work, and this method shows a certain industrial production and application value.

The improvement of TiO2 photocatalytic performance by CTAB addition and CQDs compound modification can be explained as follows: (1). After forming the compound, the CQDs evenly covered the surface of the titanium dioxide crystal; this enables the CQDs to can effectively use their conversion photoluminescence characteristics to enhance the light absorption intensity and broaden the absorption range. Thus, the even coverage is conducive to the transfer of photogenerated electrons to the CQDs structure and free conduction under the action of large π bonds. The photogenerated electrons were finally trapped by surface O2 to produce the active radical
O2 –, thus inhibiting electron recombination with holes [51,52]. (2). The special layered mesoporous morphology of LM-TiO2 was formed by CTAB modification, as presented in Fig. 8 . The specific surface area of the composite was increased, and more active sites for photocatalytic activity could be provided due to the larger specific surface of carbon quantum dots. Moreover, carbon quantum dots can enter the LM-TiO2 mesopores because of the nanometer diameter of CQDs.

In addition, multilevel reflection of light can be realized by the LM-TiO2 special porous structure and thus the light absorption rate improves. (3). CQDs modification causes a redshift of the threshold wavelength (λg) in the absorption spectrum of LM-TiO2, and the absorption intensity in the visible and near-infrared region increases because of the fluorescence upconversion effect of CQDs, which transforms long wavelength light into shorter wavelength light. (4). Electron coupling exists between the π orbital of CQDs and the conduction band of titanium dioxide, which inhibits the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, as shown in Fig. 8, Moreover, the aromatic ring of methyl orange can form a π-π conjugated structure with the π orbital of CQDs, which increases adsorption of methyl orange on the surface of titanium dioxide photocatalyst and thus improves the photocatalytic degradation effect [53,54]. In conclusion, the sensitization of CQDs can effectively improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2.

CQDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method and then compounded with LM-TiO2, prepared by the inorganic precipitation-peptization method, to obtain CQDs/LM-TiO2 composites. LM-TiO2 has a special layered mesopores morphology, which increases the specific surface area and provides more reaction sites for photocatalytic reaction. The nano structure of CQDs can make it can well compound with LM-TiO2, and thus the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 can be significantly promoted under the synergistic effect. Under the same light irradiation (metal halide lamp, 300 W) for 24 min, 0.2 wt% CQDs/LM-TiO2 exhibits an excellent photocatalytic performance, and the degradation rate of methyl orange reaches 91.04%, which is significantly higher than that of simple TiO2 and LM-TiO2. The conversion photoluminescence of CQDs improves the light absorption, and redshifts the threshold wavelength. The combination of CQDs and LM-TiO2 can promote photocatalytic reaction and expand the practical application of titanium dioxide.

Li-Yuan Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yan-Lin Han: Investigation, Software, Validation, Formal analysis. Jin-Ju Yang: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Sheng-Lian Deng: . Bi-Yu Wang: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Youth Project of Neijiang Normal University (2020QN16) and the Undergraduate Innovative Project of Neijiang Normal University (X2020023).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Charge separation at the rutile/anatase interface: a dominant factor of photocatalytic activity
Chem. Phys. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Miyagi T, Kamei M, Mitsuhashi T, et al. Charge separation at the rutile/anatase interface: a dominant factor of photocatalytic activity, Chemical Physics Letters, 2004, 390(4-6):399-402.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot solvothermal synthesis of carbon dots/Ag nanoparticles/TiO2 nanocomposites with enhanced photocatalytic performance
Ceram. Int.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang C L, Yang K, Wei X H, et al. One-pot solvothermal synthesis of carbon dots/Ag nanoparticles/TiO2 nanocomposites with enhanced photocatalytic performance, Ceramics International, 2018, 44(18):22481-22488.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel ternary TiO2/CQDs/BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) heterostructure as photocatalyst for water purification under solar irradiation
J. Solid State Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Qu X F, Liu M H, Yang J Y, et al. A novel ternary TiO2/CQDs/BiOX (X=Cl, Br, I) heterostructure as photocatalyst for water purification under solar irradiation, Journal of Solid State Chemistry, 2018, 264:77-85.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: ZnO/carbon quantum dots nanocomposites: one-step fabrication and superior photocatalytic ability for toxic gas degradation under visible light at room temperature
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yu H, Zhang H C, Huang H, et al. ZnO/carbon quantum dots nanocomposites: one-step fabrication and superior photocatalytic ability for toxic gas degradation under visible light at room temperature, New Journal of Chemistry, 2012, 36(4):1031-1035.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fe2O3/carbon quantum dots complex photocatalysts and their enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible light
Dalton Trans.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang H C, Ming H, Lian S Y, et al. Fe2O3/carbon quantum dots complex photocatalysts and their enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible light, Dalton Transactions, 2011, 40(41):10822-10825.
